ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for sugar donut; SNOMED CT coverage of specific snack foods is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium sugar donut weighing approximately 60 g, delivering around 233 kcal, 31 g carbohydrate, and 9.6 g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Sugar Donut — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo record for Sugar Donut (cr_id F11100024). All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced from HPB at confidence 0.95. Extended fatty-acid fractions and micronutrients not reported by HPB are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 18249 (Doughnuts, yeast-leavened, with creme filling) as the closest approved secondary source; values are noted where estimated.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 65 (medium) is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for doughnuts (yeast-leavened, sugar-coated). The glycemic load per serving is calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 52.09 × 0.60) / 100 ≈ 20.3 per 60 g serving. The input record cited GL of 33.9, which corresponds to a 100 g reference portion: (65 × 52.09) / 100 = 33.9; this per-100 g GL value is retained as provided and is consistent with the GI and carbohydrate data.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 288 mg/100 g combined with high refined-carbohydrate load driving insulin-mediated sodium retention and saturated fat contributing to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid regular consumption of sugar donuts; the combination of 288 mg sodium per 100 g, high refined carbohydrates, and saturated fat from deep-frying collectively elevates blood pressure risk and provides negligible potassium or fibre offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (52.1 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (2.0 g/100 g) and a medium GI of 65 producing a very high glycemic load of 33.9 per 100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid sugar donuts entirely; the 52 g refined carbohydrate per 100 g with only 2 g fibre and a GI of 65 produces a very high glycemic load that causes rapid and significant postprandial glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying introduces trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g) and saturated fat (~3.8 g/100 g), both of which raise LDL cholesterol; ultra-processed refined flour and sugar further promote dyslipidaemia and systemic inflammation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid sugar donuts; the trans fat from frying oil raises LDL cholesterol, the saturated fat content exceeds recommended limits per serving, and the ultra-processed refined carbohydrate profile promotes dyslipidaemia and vascular inflammation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation; trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g) is strongly pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid sugar donuts; deep-frying generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation, trans fat from frying oil is strongly pro-inflammatory, and the refined sugar coating further promotes inflammatory mediators.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A sugar donut is a deep-fried ring of leavened wheat dough coated with granulated sugar. The dough is made from refined wheat flour, yeast, eggs, sugar, salt, and water, then deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and immediately rolled in granulated sugar while still hot. The result is a soft, airy, slightly chewy interior with a thin, crisp, sweet exterior crust. Per 100 g, a sugar donut provides 389 kcal, 9.0 g protein, 16.0 g fat (including approximately 3.8 g saturated fat and 0.3 g trans fat from frying), 52.1 g carbohydrate, 9.0 g sugar, 2.0 g dietary fibre, and 288 mg sodium. The glycemic index is 65 (medium), but the glycemic load per 100 g is very high at 33.9, making it clinically significant for blood glucose management. Sugar donuts are widely consumed as a breakfast item or snack across Western and international food cultures and are commonly available in Singapore bakeries, convenience stores, and fast-food chains. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high refined carbohydrate content, low fibre, trans fat from deep-frying, moderate sodium, and pro-inflammatory preparation method. It is classified as ultra-processed and should be consumed only occasionally by healthy adults.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a sugar donut?
Is a sugar donut bad for blood pressure?
How much carbohydrate is in a donut?
Is a sugar donut high in fat?
Can I eat a donut if I have heart disease?
Sugar donut vs glazed donut nutrition

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients sourced directly from HPB Foodo at confidence 0.95 (exact match, cr_id F11100024). Extended fatty-acid fractions and micronutrients estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 18249 (closest approved secondary source for yeast-leavened sugar doughnut). GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database via Atkinson et al. 2008. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced directly from HPB Foodo (cr_id F11100024, confidence 0.95). No zero-macro justification is required as all core macros are non-zero. 2. Saturated fat (3.8 g), monounsaturated fat (7.1 g), polyunsaturated fat (3.9 g), trans fat (0.3 g), and all micronutrient values not reported by HPB are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 18249 (Doughnuts, yeast-leavened, with creme filling) as the closest approved secondary source; values are approximate and should be verified against a direct HPB extended-nutrient record if available. 3. vitamin_d_mcg and selenium_mcg are set to -1: detailed vitamin D and selenium data for this specific deep-fried dough product are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA SR Legacy entry 18249 as of 2026-04-16. 4. The input record listed dietary_tags as ['plant_based', 'wholegrain'] — both tags are incorrect for a sugar donut (contains eggs; made from refined flour) and have been removed. 5. The input record listed cooking_style and dish_role as null; these have been omitted from the output as the classification section captures preparation method. 6. GI glycemic load of 33.9 in the input corresponds to a per-100 g reference calculation (65 × 52.09 / 100 = 33.9) and is retained as provided. 7. The food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; therefore no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under G6/Q6 multilingual rules. Local_names fields are set to null accordingly.

Added saturated_fat_g (3.8), monounsaturated_fat_g (7.1), polyunsaturated_fat_g (3.9), trans_fat_g (0.3) estimated from USDA SR Legacy 18249.
Added full micronutrient panel estimated from USDA SR Legacy 18249; vitamin_d_mcg and selenium_mcg set to -1 with documentation.
Replaced input health_suitability (diabetes/hypertension/rheumatoid_arthritis) with RESULT template health_context structure including required Type 2 Diabetes and Cardiovascular Disease conditions.
Corrected GI source from 'Embedded GI Database' (non-approved) to University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008.
Added comprehensive aliases and aliases_by_language with consistency self-check performed.
Added FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology anchors; SNOMED CT set to null with gap note.
Added serving section, classification section, and full llm_training section.
